breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: Inside serverQuery select new; materialized at serverQuery.Count() (L243) and serverQuery.ToList() (L247), no boundary before projection. CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression.
finalFix:
In Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs L224, replace the CLR shim call with EF-native DateTime.AddDays, which EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD and preserves the legacy EF6 semantics. PODate is non-nullable DateTime so no .Value on it; POValidity is int? so the non-null ternary branch uses .Value. Preserve the legacy comparison direction (DateTime.Now <= AddDays(...)) by writing it as AddDays(...) >= DateTime.Now:

validity = (b.PODate.AddDays((b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.POValidity + 1).Value) >= DateTime.Now) ? "Active" : "Expired",

Note: the same pattern appears in a second branch of the legacy file (L347) and likely a corresponding second branch in the ported file (the controller has the `if (project != null)` / else split, mirroring legacy L221 and L347) â€” apply the identical fix to that branch too if present.
reasoning: CONFIRMED â€” genuine EF Core 10 port-break. (a) SERVER-SIDE: serverQuery is built directly from db.PurchaseOrders (IQueryable) at Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs L176 via a LINQ query expression. The `select new { ... validity = ... }` projection (L202-235) has NO .ToList()/.AsEnumerable()/.AsAsyncEnumerable() boundary before it. The query is first enumerated server-side at serverQuery.Count() (L243) and serverQuery.ToList() (L247). Only AFTER L247 does client-side work begin (serverRows.Select, lookups). So the L224 fragment is unambiguously inside the unmaterialized server query â€” not after a boundary, not a standalone top-level local, not a group-aggregate. (b) WOULD 500: validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.PODate, (b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : b.POValidity + 1)) ? \"Active\" : \"Expired\". DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays is a plain CLR static helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (L70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`) â€” it is NOT a registered/mapped EF DbFunction and has no translation registration. EF Core 10 cannot translate this method call (nor the null-propagating `?.AddDays`) into SQL; it throws InvalidOperationException 'The LINQ expression ... could not be translated' on enumeration â†’ HTTP 500. This fires on ANY row returned by the grid (realistic data), so severity = HIGH. PORT REGRESSION: legacy EF6 at _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs L221 used `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.PODate, ...)` (System.Data.Entity.DbFunctions.AddDays = canonical function â†’ SQL DATEADD), which EF6 translated fine. The port swapped the canonical EF6 function for an untranslatable CLR shim. The hunter's class and location are correct. One refinement to the hunter's proposed fix: b.PODate is non-nullable DateTime (Models/PurchaseOrder.cs L17) so it needs NO .Value, and b.POValidity is int? (L46) so the non-null ternary branch must use .Value to satisfy the EF-native DateTime.AddDays(double) overload (which takes a non-nullable arg). The hunter's `(b.POValidity + 1).Value` is correct on that point but `b.PODate.Value` is wrong (PODate is non-nullable).

breakClass: DateTime arithmetic helper inside an unmaterialized query: DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays/AddMinutes (plain CLR helpers in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs, NOT registered EF functions) applied to a COLUMN expression inside a server-side LINQ-to-Entities projection or Where. EF6 used DbFunctions.AddDays (canonical -> SQL DATEADD); the port swapped in DbFunctionsCompat.* which EF Core 10 cannot translate, so the query throws 'could not be translated' -> HTTP 500. Fix = EF-native (col).Value.AddDays(n ?? 0) which DOES translate to DATEADD.
why: Second DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays projection in same controller (parallel grid/method). Same server-side select-new pattern; CLR helper untranslatable -> 500. Legacy DbFunctions.AddDays. Port regression. (Verify enclosing query's materialization mirrors L224's; both are register-grid projections.)
finalFix:
In Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs line 381 (the `else`-branch grid projection), replace the untranslatable CLR-shim call with the EF-native DateTime.AddDays (which EF Core 10 translates to SQL DATEADD, mirroring legacy DbFunctions.AddDays). Because PODate is non-nullable DateTime (NO `.Value`) and POValidity is int? (days arg must be non-nullable int):

validity = (DateTime.Now <= b.PODate.AddDays((b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : (b.POValidity + 1).Value)) ? "Active" : "Expired",

(Apply the identical fix to the first occurrence at line 224 in the `if (project != null)` branch â€” same pattern, same 500.)

Rationale vs the claim's proposed fix: do NOT use `b.PODate.Value.AddDays(...)` â€” PODate is a non-nullable DateTime so `.Value` won't compile. Keeping `DateTime.Now <= ...` (original direction) instead of flipping to `>= DateTime.Now` also avoids any edge-equality drift, preserving exact legacy output.
reasoning:
CONFIRMED as a genuine EF Core 10 port-break. I read Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs around line 381 and traced the full query.

(a) SERVER-SIDE: The query is built at line 335 as `var serverQuery = (from b in db.PurchaseOrders join ... select new { ... validity = (DateTime.Now <= DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays(b.PODate, (b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : b.POValidity + 1)) ? "Active" : "Expired", ... });` assigned to an IQueryable. The `select new` projection (incl. the `validity` expression at line 381) closes at line 392. The query is first enumerated at line 400 (`serverQuery.Count()` under fastPage) and line 404 (`serverQuery.ToList()`). There is NO `.ToList()`/`.AsEnumerable()`/`.AsAsyncEnumerable()` boundary before the projection, and it is not a group-aggregate over an IGrouping. So the `validity` expression is translated into SQL â€” genuinely server-side. (This is the SECOND occurrence; the first identical one is line 224 in the `if (project != null)` branch. Line 381 is the `else`-branch parallel register-grid projection.)

(b) WOULD 500: `DbFunctionsCompat.AddDays` is a plain CLR helper in Helpers/SendMailShim.cs (line 70: `public static DateTime? AddDays(DateTime? date, int? days) => date?.AddDays(days ?? 0);`), explicitly NOT a registered EF function (its own comment says "client-evaluated"). EF Core 10 cannot translate a call to an arbitrary static CLR method inside a server-side projection, so query compilation throws InvalidOperationException "could not be translated" -> HTTP 500. This 500s unconditionally for ANY data (even one row), so it is HIGH severity â€” the grid endpoint cannot load at all once this `else` branch executes.

PORT REGRESSION: Confirmed against legacy. _archive/legacy-mvc5/Controllers/PurchaseOrderController.cs:347 uses `DbFunctions.AddDays(b.PODate, (b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : b.POValidity + 1)` â€” the EF6 canonical function that DOES translate to SQL DATEADD and ran fine in EF6. The port swapped in the untranslatable CLR shim. Same registers-grid projection; net regression.

NOTE ON CLAIM'S PROPOSED FIX (it has a compile bug): The hunter proposed `b.PODate.Value.AddDays(...)`. But `PODate` is a NON-NULLABLE `DateTime` (Models/PurchaseOrder.cs:17 `public DateTime PODate`), so `.Value` does not exist on it and that expression will NOT compile. The correct output-preserving fix drops `.Value` on PODate (it's already DateTime) and coalesces the nullable days to int. `POValidity` is `int?` (Models/PurchaseOrder.cs:46), so `(b.POValidity == null) ? 0 : b.POValidity + 1` is `int?` and must be made non-nullable.

Entity types verified: PurchaseOrder.PODate = DateTime (non-nullable), PurchaseOrder.POValidity = int?.
